## Considerations in High Dimensions
This document explores the common problems that may arise when using high-dimensional datasets, in which the number of features is larger than the number of observations. 

#### Concept to clarify: multicollinearity and its impact on interpretation and prediction
A concept I wanted to explore and clarify was the how multicollinearity in high-dimensional settings where any variable in the model can be written as a linear combination of all of the other variables in the model impacts our interpretation and prediction abilities.

#### Prompt
I am an environmental data scientist who taking a machine learning course, and I am reading about the problems that may arise in high-dimensional datasets. It was stated that "In Chapter 3, we learned about multi-collinearity, the concept that the variables in a regression might be correlated with each other. In the high-dimensional setting, the multicollinearity problem is extreme: any variable in the model can be written as a linear combination of all of the other variables in the model. Essentially, this means that we can never know exactly which variables (if any) truly are predictive of the outcome, and we can never identify the best coefficients for use in the regression. At most, we can hope to assign large regression coefficients to variables that are correlated with the variables that truly are predictive of the outcome. For instance, suppose that we are trying to predict blood pressure on the basis of half a million SNPs, and that forward stepwise selection indicates that 17 of those SNPs lead to a good predictive model on the training data. It would be incorrect to conclude that these 17 SNPs predict blood pressure more effectively than the other SNPs not included in the model. There are likely to be many sets of 17 SNPs that would predict blood pressure just as well as the selected model." I would like to understand how multicolliniearity affects our ability to interpret and predict from a model. Provide a parallel example from environmental data science where the same interpretation vs. prediction tradeoff would arise.

#### Response
Environmental data science parallel:
Suppose you're predicting air quality (PM2.5 levels) across a city using hundreds of remotely sensed variables — land surface temperature, NDVI, impervious surface cover, albedo, distance to roads, etc. Many of these are highly correlated: urban areas with lots of impervious surface tend to have high temperatures, low vegetation, and poor air quality all at once.
A Lasso model might select land surface temperature and impervious surface as the top predictors and predict PM2.5 accurately. But you can't conclude those are the true drivers of poor air quality — NDVI or road proximity would likely produce an equally good model. If a city planner asks "what should we change to improve air quality?", the model coefficients can't reliably answer that. For prediction it works; for actionable environmental policy, you'd need additional causal analysis.


#### Generative AI reflection
I used claude to answer my prompt, and it took two iterations to get it to clarify the question, because at first it just told me that it was a well-structured prompt and how I could improve it. I had to ask it to actually answer it after that. The response matched to information given in the textbook pretty well, and it created a good analogy for the question in the context of environmental data science compared to the SNP example. I think that using AI to generate examples and illustrations of concepts could be helpful in the future.
